role_summary: UCLA student and 1961 Freedom Rider (arrested and severely beaten in Houston, Texas); chairman of the UCLA political group Platform; UCLA representative to the National Student Association (NSA) in 1963; National Civil Rights Director of the National Student Association in the mid-1960s; participant in the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party's Congressional Challenge Strategy Committee; civil-rights program officer at the Ford Foundation (1967-1968); subsequent career as employment-law attorney in Los Angeles.
ai_reading: McNichols's top semantic neighbor is C. T. Vivian (entry 134) at a high cosine count of 201, a strong single-neighbor signature in this catalog. The Vivian pairing is the catalog's clearest cross-region Freedom-Rider-and-direct-action-trainer cosine: Vivian was one of the SCLC field staff who participated in the Freedom Rides and who trained the Nashville-based riders; McNichols was a UCLA-based rider who joined the broader 1961 network. The novel observation his page surfaces is the West-Coast-student-into-national-civil-rights-staff signature: McNichols's UCLA-Platform / NSA / Ford Foundation career arc is the catalog's clearest case of a Freedom-Rider-to-institutional-foundation-staff trajectory that the historiography often subordinates to the more-photographed Mississippi-and-Alabama field-organizer biographies.
biographical_paragraph: While McNichols's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), his external biographical record is documented in the Veterans of the Civil Rights Movement archive [src: 1, src: 2]. Steven Everett McNichols was a UCLA undergraduate from New York when he joined the 1961 Freedom Rides; he was arrested at the Houston bus station and severely beaten by a white mob [src: 2]. He returned to UCLA after the Rides and led Platform, the early-1960s UCLA political organization; he was elected UCLA's representative to the National Student Association in 1963 and rose to head the NSA's Civil Rights Division in the mid-1960s. In that role he was a participant in the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party's Congressional Challenge Strategy Committee (the 1964-1965 effort to unseat the all-white Mississippi congressional delegation) [src: 2]. He worked at the Ford Foundation in 1967-1968 as a program officer making grants to civil rights organizations and leaders, earned a Master's in economics at the University of Texas, worked for Los Angeles Mayor Tom Bradley, and built a subsequent career as an attorney specializing in plaintiff's employment law in California [src: 2].
